data.title: Janus Henderson Mid Cap Growth Managed Account Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Portfolio returned 4.36% (gross) and the Russell Midcap® Growth Index returned 2.78%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the information technology sector contributed to relative performance, while stock selection in the healthcare sector detracted.

Outlook

We remain committed to a disciplined, bottom-up investment approach that pursues growth opportunities while seeking to manage downside risk.

Investment environment

  • U.S. shares rose on positive corporate earnings news and hopes for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts. The Fed reduced its policy rate by 25 basis points in September, with the market expecting at least one additional rate cut by year-end.
  • Economic growth has been relatively stable despite policy headwinds and slower-than-expected employment growth.
  • Excitement around artificial intelligence (AI) was a strong driver of market gains, especially in sectors such as communication services. The defensive consumer staples sector lagged the broader market.

Portfolio review

Excitement around AI was a positive tailwind for several holdings. These included AppLovin (APP), the developer and owner of a mobile marketing platform that matches developers with advertisers looking to be featured on apps and digital games. This platform utilizes AI to improve match rates and returns on ad spending. The company's strong second-quarter earnings growth gave investors increased confidence in its business model and growth. AppLovin is also rolling out a new program that will expose its platform to a broader ecosystem of e-commerce advertisers, increasing its revenue opportunities. We trimmed the stock after its very strong performance, but continue to believe in AppLovin's growth potential.

Diversified electronics manufacturer Flex (FLEX) was another contributor. Flex has become a critical enabler of the data center buildout by producing customized power and cooling equipment for hyperscalers and large technology companies, providing a cost-effective alternative to branded equipment. This business has been very profitable for Flex, and we believe the company is positioned to expand its share of the data center market while capitalizing on growing AI-related capital investment.

Among individual holdings, CarMax (KMX) was a relative detractor. CarMax has become the leading U.S. used automotive retailer through its strong value proposition and multichannel sales strategy. Shares declined after CarMax reported weaker-than-expected second-quarter earnings that reflected economic pressures on vehicle sales. While we were disappointed with these results, we believe in the company's long-term potential, including its ongoing migration to a digital-first strategy that may help drive sales, reduce overhead, and improve margins.

LPL Financial (LPLA), another detractor, provides a full-service, technology-enabled investment platform that helps independent financial advisors serve clients and improve business returns. While LPL Financial reported strong third-quarter results, the stock declined on concerns that lower interest rates could reduce the float income it earns on client accounts. We viewed this decline as an overreaction, and we remain upbeat on long-term potential for LPL as it has continued to grow its market share and expand its asset base.

Manager outlook

We have been encouraged by the resilience of the economy and the performance of our growth investments. At the same time, we acknowledge economic crosscurrents, including slower employment growth and the potential impact of tariffs on inflation and spending. While such crosswinds may complicate the Fed's task, we believe lower interest rates could provide an economic tailwind that could benefit a broad range of companies. Equity valuations are expensive by historical standards, however, which could make markets vulnerable to any downward projections in growth. We would also welcome some market broadening as we remain concerned that recent equity market performance has been narrow, where gains have been fueled primarily by the appreciation of AI-related stocks.

We continue to view AI as an important investment theme that has the potential to be a powerful driver of long-term economic growth. At the same time, we recognize the uncertainties associated with any new technology, as well as the likelihood of winners and losers as the market matures. In pursuing AI-related opportunities, we have focused on companies with unique market positions and competitive advantages that appear durable. We also remain committed to our disciplined bottom-up approach, and will only invest in companies with demonstrated profit models, proven management teams, strong balance sheets, and valuations that we believe are supported by expected earnings growth. As diversified portfolio managers, we have also pursued opportunities beyond AI, including around secular growth trends such as healthcare innovation or reshoring. We have also sought underappreciated growth companies that we believe offer solid earnings growth potential over the next three to five years but that trade at much lower earnings multiples relative to AI-related names. We believe this diversified, risk-aware approach, which pursues a broad range of growth opportunities, should allow us to perform in a variety of market environments while also managing downside risk.

Portfolio management: Brian Demain, CFA, Phillip Cody Wheaton, CFA

data.title: Columbia Seligman Technology And Information Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Seligman Technology and Information Fund returned 25.75% for the third quarter.
  • The fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P North American Technology Index, which returned 13.14% for the quarter.
  • Stock selection within semiconductors and semiconductor equipment, as well as technology hardware storage & peripherals, contributed positively to performance during the quarter. The fund's off-benchmark allocation to industrials also contributed significantly to performance relative to the S&P North American Technology Sector Index. The fund's allocation to financials and stock selection within communication services detracted from relative returns.
  • For monthly performance, please visit columbiathreadneedleus.com .

Market overview

U.S. equities moved steadily higher during the third quarter, building on gains from the first six months of the year. The Russell 1000 Index — a measure of performance for large-cap stocks — rose 7.99%, boosting its year-to-date return to 14.60% as of September 30. Investors were cheered by a continuation of positive economic growth, receding fears about the possible impact of tariffs and robust second-quarter corporate earnings. A favorable shift in U.S. Federal Reserve policy provided an additional tailwind for the market. The Fed reduced rates by a quarter point at its September meeting, its first cut of 2025, and investors began to anticipate that further easing would be in store over the coming 12 months. This environment fueled a surge in investor appetite for risk, leading to a rally highlighted by a series of new all-time highs for the major indices.

Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

Quarterly portfolio recap

  • Technology indices continued to move higher during the quarter, as evidenced by the S&P North American Technology Sector Index, which returned 13.14% during the third quarter. The primary driver of returns in the technology market has been companies indexed to artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure and the cloud, while other sectors of technology have struggled to keep pace. The rate of capital expenditure (CAPEX) spending remains high, as cloud providers have already committed to significant spending in 2026, on the back of historically high 2025 spending. U.S. cloud-data-center spending from Meta, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon (AMZN), CoreWeave, Apple and Oracle (ORCL) is expected to exceed $400 billion in 2025 alone.
  • The fund maintained an underweight position in NVIDIA relative to its benchmark. The chip manufacturer represents a large allocation in the benchmark. The fund's underweight position detracted slightly from relative returns as the company posted positive results and traded higher during the quarter, in line with other AI-indexed securities.
  • The portfolio's holding in semiconductor equipment manufacturer Lam Research contributed significantly to performance as the stock performed exceptionally well during the quarter and the fund maintained a significant relative overweight to the benchmark. Lam continues to be an AI beneficiary from a company perspective, as AI workloads require more advanced chips, which drives demand for Lam Research's deposition and etch solutions. The rebounding NAND flash market is also contributing to the growth of the company, as Lam's molybdenum and carbon gap fill solutions are continuing to gain traction. The company announced record-high profits and revenues on the company's most recent earnings call.
  • The fund continued to hold an off-benchmark position in Bloom Energy. The company manufactures and markets solid-oxide fuel cells that produce electricity and can provide an alternative source of energy compared to traditional supply. Our thesis on Bloom has been that it has the necessary technology to provide a solution to the electricity shortage that overhangs new AI data center builds in the U.S. and around the world. Bloom's fuel cells plug into a natural gas line that can fit on a data center's campus without taking up much real estate, and the company has informed customers they can ramp up power delivery quicker than other energy providers. Bloom received a favorable tax credit from the Trump administration's "One Big Beautiful Bill," which included a provision that supports hydrogen fuel cell and clean hydrogen production. This credit has led Bloom to approach cost parity with many other energy providers, including wind turbines. Add in the company's ability to deliver a solution quicker, and the current environment appears set up well for Bloom. In July, the company also announced a partnership with Oracle to provide support for Oracle's OCI data-center business and said they could be operational within 90-days - further emphasizing our thesis on Bloom's opportunity due to traditional energy backlogs.
  • The fund maintained a position in Apple throughout the quarter, at an underweight relative to the benchmark that detracted from relative performance. Apple was at the center of a few headlines during the quarter. In particular, the company received a ruling in its case with Google that Google can continue to pay Apple marketing fees to remain a search engine in the Safari browser, although not the exclusive search engine. Apple also benefited from a meeting with President Trump in August in which he announced that Apple would be largely exempt from paying tariffs because the company has been investing in the United States. Within hardware, the fund also maintained an overweight position in Western Digital that helped relative performance versus the benchmark. Fundamentally, the company is well positioned in its space and should benefit from being a downstream AI beneficiary, and from the increased cost of memory. The company has increased its margins and recently raised the prices of its disk drives for the first time in a few years, signaling continued strong demand for its products.

  • Within software, the fund's performance was mixed. Some large positions contributed to performance, but there were positions the fund owned at an overweight relative to the benchmark that ultimately detracted. The fund held an underweight position in Microsoft which contributed positively to performance as the stock moved slightly higher during the third quarter. The fund's position in cloud provider Oracle also contributed to performance relative to the benchmark during the third quarter. Oracle saw a large run-up in its stock as the company announced that its remaining performance obligations jumped year over year and also announced new partnerships with Google and OpenAI during the quarter. The fund did not hold a position in Palantir Technologies (PLTR). This detracted from performance as Palantir's stock price rose during the quarter, driven by new contracts the company secured with the U.S. government, including the Department of Defense and Immigration and Customs Enforcement. The fund also did not hold a position in AppLovin (APP), which detracted from returns relative to the index. AppLovin's stock moved higher during the quarter, fueled by optimism ahead of a product event that unveiled a self-serve ad platform aimed at ecommerce and other non-gaming advertisers.
  • The fund maintained an overweight position relative to the market in semiconductor company Broadcom that contributed positively to performance. Broadcom's expertise in designing custom data-center chips, application-specific integrated circuits (ASIC) and auxiliary processing units (XPU), for major hyperscale customers like Google and Meta drove significant growth for the company during the third quarter. Broadcom also announced a new partnership with OpenAI that brings their four largest customers to Alphabet, Meta, ByteDance and OpenAI. The fund's overweight position in semiconductor company Marvell Technology also contributed positively to performance, as Marvell's stock price continued to recover from April lows. The fund's allocations to Teradyne (TER) and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) also contributed positively to performance, as the semiconductor industry as a whole continued to recover well behind subdued tariff news. The fund's overweight positions in ON Semiconductor (ON) and NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) detracted from performance relative to the benchmark as non-AI-indexed semiconductor companies continued to see a lag in performance relative to AI-indexed firms. The fund did not hold positions in Intel (INTC) or Micron Technology (MU), which detracted from performance as both names posted positive results during the quarter with their stock prices moving higher.

Outlook

The market is navigating significant crosscurrents of positive and negative trends, as we have seen the market continue to rebound off lows driven primarily by tariff announcements earlier this year. Positive market trends include:

  • Hyperscaler capex spending remains strong, and executives are committed to participating in the AI race.
  • Semiconductor chip demand remains robust, and the need for higher bandwidth memory has increased memory pricing, as AI demand levels are still elevated.
  • The need for robust cybersecurity solutions has become paramount as the focus on AI increases, and cybersecurity firms have been able to find some pockets of success despite overall volatility within technology

The negatives include:

  • Information technology (IT) service firms are facing difficulties, as some investors expect AI to replace call-center employees and IT consultants.
  • Financial technology (Fintech) has struggled during recent quarters, due to fears of weakening consumer spending amid car-loan delinquencies and pressure from student loans becoming payable again.
  • Enterprise software continues to be negatively impacted by announcements from AI providers of new programs that could disrupt traditional software providers.

Our expectations for various sectors are as follows:

  • AI remains the strongest theme in technology, as infrastructure buildouts continue and companies hasten to deploy new AI capabilities. Capital spending by cloud service providers including Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Oracle and Meta Platforms (META) continue to grow.
  • Semiconductor equipment demand has continued to recover strongly during the second and third quarters behind quieting tariff news. Semiconductor equipment companies have been focused on reallocating production across geographic locations to adjust for potential impacts of tariffs once conversations restart.
  • Cybersecurity consistently remains a top priority for chief information officer (CIO) budgets, with limited impact from macro factors, and companies positioned here continue to have a favorable secular backdrop for sustained growth.

data.title: BillionToOne: An Interesting Set-Up Into 2026
data.description:
In November, I believed that shares of BillionToOne (BLLN) enjoyed a well-deserved opening day boom. Leveraging groundbreaking molecular diagnostic technology for prenatal and oncology testing markets, BillionToOne saw spectacular and, moreover, profitable growth.

Forwarding to year-end, shares have lost a quarter of their value, or a bit more if we look at the operating asset valuation. This followed a solid third quarter, and while the fourth quarter guidance feels a bit soft, it might easily be conservative as well.

Balancing all this, I am getting cautiously optimistic here, looking to learn more about this interesting business heading into 2026.

Removing The Fear Of The Unknown

BillionToOne develops technologies that push beyond limitations in diagnostics, being able to detect diseases with just a single molecule at a time.

The business was born after its founders asked a single question: what is the single most challenging and yet impactful global healthcare problem out there, to be solved with physics, that is not addressed by other players?

The name of the company is a bit awkward, with practical considerations at the time of its founding allowing for the domain name to be bought for just $12. However, the business is dead serious. Its technology allows for the development of prenatal and oncology test products, which create actionable insights from a single blood draw, furthermore allowing for personalized medication opportunities as well.

Its prenatal UNITY test was launched in 2019 and used to provide risk assessment for sickle cell disease and cystic fibrosis, otherwise typically requiring larger blood samples and/or invasive procedures. In 2023, the company launched the Northstar Select and Response tests, two pan-cancer liquid biopsy tests.

These tests have been performed over a million times to date. Further growth was seen, also as the technology can be extended to other tests as well, at least that are the plans for the coming years.

Valuation Thoughts

BillionToOne went public in November of this year, with shares priced at $60 per share to the public, well above the preliminary pricing range. Following the offering, the company was set to operate with about a $400 million net cash position, by my math.

Shares doubled to $120 on the first day of trading, with some 45 million shares outstanding, resulting in a $5.0 billion operating asset valuation. This is a huge number for a business that generated $71 million in sales in 2023, although it was accompanied by an operating loss of $69 million. Revenues more than doubled to $152 million in 2024, as operating losses narrowed to $47 million.

Revenues rose by another 81% in the first half of the year, reported at $125 million, for a $250 million run rate. Moreover, an operating loss of $4 million revealed that profits were in sight, although expectations had risen substantially on the first day of trading.

The company guided for preliminary third-quarter sales at $83 million, indicating that annual growth re-accelerated to more than 100% again, with income seen at $8.5 million. Some 90% of these sales were generated from prenatal tests, complemented by $8.5 million in oncology revenues and some smaller service components. With revenues trending at just $330 million, shares traded around 15 times annualized and profitable sales.

Nonetheless, given the incredible momentum seen, I decided to err on the cautious side, not just given the momentum but also other risks related to competition, reliance on key staff, etc.

Coming In

Since the offering early in November, shares of BillionToOne have sold off quite a bit to the current level of $87 per share, trading right in between the official IPO price and levels on the opening day of trading.

Early in December, the company reported a 117% increase in third-quarter sales to $83.5 million, largely in line with preliminary numbers. Prenatal revenues rose by 102% to $74.1 million as oncology revenues increased sevenfold to $8.7 million, with all these revenues generated from about 163,000 tests fetching an average selling price of around $500. Operating income was reported at $9.6 million, allowing for some real profits here.

With about 45 million shares trading at $87, the market value of the firm stands at just over $3.9 billion, including a nearly half a billion cash position, for a $3.4 billion enterprise valuation. While the third quarter results look quite compelling, the issue is that fourth quarter sales are only seen up to $84-$90 million, with annual growth slowing down to 86-100%. The company only indicated that GAAP operating profits were expected but failed to further quantify this.

That is not surprising given the third quarter results. Note that growth in the third quarter was split pretty evenly between growth in average selling prices and the number of tests performed.

In between all these developments, the actual sales multiple applied to operating assets has fallen to 10 times sales, based on the fourth quarter revenue guidance.

This is very comforting given the potential of the business, although sequential revenue growth is slowing down too if we have to believe the outlook. However, this could easily be conservative as well, as that is not too uncommon for new management teams. Often teams tend to guide conservatively soon after an offering, something that I do not rule out here as well.

Other good news is that the company has reached a deal with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) to establish an exclusive agreement for hemolytic diseases of fetuses and newborns. Moreover, the company announced very positive study results, as published in scientific journals, with all of this anecdotal news flow being solid, helping to instill confidence here.

Currently, no 2026 guidance or forecast has been given here, but frankly, the situation looks quite healthy. While the fourth-quarter guidance is soft, shares have come down quite a bit, lowering expectations along the way.

While the situation is highly uncertain and the company is largely a one-trick pony, valuations look more reasonable here. Of course, there are above-average risks, yet the potential of the business is evident as well, as I look forward to holding a small position here, hoping to learn more about the business into 2026 and its corporate developments.

data.title: Goldman Sachs International Equity Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The MSCI EAFE Index returned +4.77% in Q3 2025 and has returned +25.14% YTD.

Eurozone equity markets posted gains in Q3 2025, with Financials and Health Care sectors driving the advance. In contrast, Telecoms and Communication Services underperformed. Bank stocks were notably supported by robust corporate earnings.

The Services sector showed expansion in Germany, Italy, and Spain, while France lagged. Foreign demand remained subdued, as evidenced by a continued decline in new export orders for the 28th consecutive month—highlighting persistent challenges in global trade, according to the Hamburg Commercial Bank (HCOB) flash eurozone Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) for September. European Central Bank (ECB) President Christine Lagarde confirmed that the sharp inflation surge from 2022 to 2024 has eased, and inflation risks are now balanced. Inflation met the ECB's 2% target in August, though September figures are expected to slightly exceed it. Despite trade tariffs imposed by the US, Lagarde noted the eurozone has weathered the impact better than anticipated, experiencing only moderate growth effects and limited inflationary pressure.

UK equities delivered a strong performance, with the FTSE 100 recording its best quarter since late 2022. A resilient global economy contributed to gains, while a weaker pound benefited internationally oriented firms. Communication services and technology sectors excelled, fueled by ongoing enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI). Basic materials also rallied, supported by rising gold prices. The London Stock Exchange saw renewed activity in IPOs. UK inflation remained elevated at 3.8% in August, driven by food, energy, and regulated utility costs. In response, the Bank of England's (BOE) Monetary Policy Committee narrowly voted to cut the Bank Rate by 0.25 percentage points to 4.0%—marking its first rate reduction since 2020. In September, the BoE also announced a slowdown in its quantitative tightening program, a move aimed at easing bond yields and borrowing costs.

Japanese equities regained their positive upwards momentum in the third quarter delivering 11% total returns on the back of resilient domestic economy, the ongoing corporate reforms and the expectations of a US rate cut. The US-Japan trade negotiations reached a favorable outcome in July 2025 bringing down the US tariffs on Japanese exports from 25% to 15% and triggering a strong rally led by exporters and other cyclical industries. The 2Q25 real GDP growth exceeded market expectations as domestic consumption grew for fifth straight quarter, albeit at a moderate pace, and capex accelerated further. The core Consumer Price Index (CPI) numbers slowed down sharply to 2.5% driven by decelerating rice prices, electricity/gas control measures and some special measures for childcare. The corporate earnings trends for TOPIX remained in positive territory, particularly for cyclicals driven by the readjustment of tariffs assumptions and for AI linked component manufacturers given the strong demand expectations for data centers.

At the sector level, stock selection in Financials and Materials were the greatest contributors to performance whereas Industrials and Communication Services were the largest detractors.

BBVA (3.56%) , a Spanish multinational financial services company, was the greatest contributor to performance over the quarter. The company posted strong earnings momentum supported by growth in recurring revenues and net interest income. The stock also benefitted from supportive tailwinds in the European banking industry. We are constructive on BBVA (BBVA)'s global footprint with strong growth optionality in Mexico in particular. We expect the bank to continue its share repurchase programs due to its strong organic capital generation.

Murata Manufacturing (2.07%) , a Japanese manufacturer of electronic components, was another contributor to performance. The company unveiled innovative products in energy efficiency and smart device integration which drove the stock over the quarter. There were also signals of increased demand for the company's AI and green energy products. We believe Murata is one of the market leaders for several key components in smartphones, PCs and autos and is well positioned to benefit from increased component content due to the proliferation of AI and EVs.

Capgemini (1.69%) , a French IT services and engineering company, was the largest detractor in Q3. The company has suffered from being perceived as an "AI Loser" as investors fear its services may be disrupted by AI. The company's stock price declined during July following an unexpected reduction in its annual revenue forecast, attributed to diminished demand within the automotive and aerospace industries. Investor apprehension was heightened as management communicated a cautious outlook and indicated that market recovery may proceed more slowly than anticipated. However, we believe that as a leading IT services provider, Capgemini (CGEMY) is well positioned to benefit from long-term structural global demand for Digital transformation, Cloud migration and Managed services. The company also has a strong Consulting arm that works closely with its customer leadership to ensure that Capgemini is at the forefront of emerging multi-year industry trends.

Deutsche Telekom (3.16%) , a telecommunications services provider, was another detractor to performance over the month. The stock declined due to weakness in its domestic business with competitive pressures leading to loss of subscribers and below expectations revenues. The stock also declined after Deutsche Telekom (DTEGY)'s sale of $46.2 MM T Mobile US shares, suggesting a change in its capital structure. Nonetheless we believe Deutsche Telekom presents a rare structural growth story in the telecom sector, driven by the strong performance of T-Mobile US (TMUS). We believe the company has ample headroom to both enhance its growth outlook, supported by prudent bets (like German and US fiber and tower Mergers & Acquisitions and simultaneously continue enhancing shareholder returns.

We initiated a position in Industria de Diseno Textil (Inditex) (IDEXY) (2.03%), a Spanish fashion company that offers wide range of women's clothing. We see Inditex as a high-quality compounder with a strong cash generation track record and attractive total shareholder returns.

We initiated a position in Accor (1.69%), a French chain hospitality company. The Company owns, manages, and franchises hotels, resorts, and vacation facilities. We believe Accor can grow revenues above consensus over the next 3 years and drive EBITDA margin expansion. We believe there is a re-rating opportunity as the company becomes less complex, increasingly asset light, sees underlying asset quality improvements, and more predictable in line with leading hotel peer groups.

We exited our position in Nestle (NSRGY) (0.00%), a large Swiss food and beverage company. In September, the company unexpectedly terminated its CEO Laurent Freixe due to a breach in conduct. Having observed strong performance from the Dutch telecommunications and IT provider Royal KPN (KKPNY) (0.00%), we have chosen to exit our position in line with our sell discipline. Markets have experienced plenty of volatility due to economic, political, and geopolitical uncertainty and may continue to do so through the end of the year. New realities that took shape at the start of the year – including higher for longer interest rates, elevated geopolitical risk, and megatrends rapidly transforming industries – continue to create a complex environment of evolving opportunities and risks in global markets. We believe there may be opportunities to broaden equity exposures beyond the largest US names and into international markets. For instance, we are constructive on the structural economic drivers in Europe and corporate governance reforms in Japanese equity markets.

As active investors, we select companies because of our confidence in their ability to grow, and prosper relative to their competitors, over the economic cycle. We are fundamental investors and will remain focused on the long-term rather than trying to time the ups and downs of short-term market gyrations.

data.title: Hyster-Yale Is Facing Too Many Challenges To Upgrade
data.description:
One company that I find to be particularly fascinating from an operational standpoint is Hyster-Yale, Inc. (HY). If you're not familiar with the business, it essentially offers customers acquisition-tailored lift trucks and solutions. This is the kind of industry that I find appealing. And in the past, the company had achieved some rather remarkable growth.

Lately, however, things have shifted. Weaker economic conditions have weighed on the business. Backlog has been plummeting, and shares are no longer as cheap as they were from a valuation standpoint. At the end of the day, I have taken a bearish stance on the business even though it has pained me to do so.

That bearish stance has paid off. Since I last reaffirmed the company as a Sell candidate back in September of this year, the stock has dropped 12.9%. That's at a time when the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 3.5%. But that's not all. You see, whenever I rate a company a Sell, I don't necessarily expect the share price to fall. I just expect the company to materially underperform the market. And since I originally rated it a Sell back in September of 2022, the stock has appreciated by only 59.3%. Over that same window of time, the market is up 86.7%. Now with shares having dropped as much as they have, I believe that reevaluating the picture is appropriate. Unfortunately, the big thing standing between it and an upgrade is the fact that backlog continues to experience downward pressure. We have seen a little stabilization when it comes to bookings. And that could be a sign that some of the worst pressures are over. But for now, I believe that containing this as a soft Sell, especially in light of broader economic concerns, would be appropriate.

Too early for an upgrade

Given how recently I have written about Hyster-Yale, we only have data covering one additional operating quarter. And that happens to be the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company came in at $979.1 million. That's a drop from the $1.02 billion the company reported a year earlier. This decline was driven in large part by its lift truck business, with revenue declining 4% from $967.8 million to $929.3 million. Although there was an increase in revenue in the EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) regions, the Americas and JAPIC (what it calls its Asia Pacific regions), saw declines.

In the Americas, revenue dropped 5%. This was driven entirely by a decline in unit volume and changes in product mix. That affected revenue negatively to the tune of $77.4 million. And according to management, this can be chalked up to cautious customers because of "ongoing economic uncertainty with increased pressure from low cost foreign competitors."

In essence, this means that the economic climate is unfavorable at the moment and low-cost competitors from overseas are eating its lunch. The JAPIC region for the company fared even worse. Revenue dropped 9.4%, from $51.3 million to $46.5 million. This decline seems to have been caused by multiple factors, including foreign currency fluctuations, a change in parts, and other items. But the big issue ended up being unit volume and product mix that, once again, was driven by the issues that I highlighted.

There's also the Bolzoni segment, which focuses on producing and distributing attachments, forks, and lift tables. Revenue here plunged 10.9%, from $97.6 million to $87 million. According to management, this was also driven by lower unit volume. Considering the broader economic climate, this does not surprise me in the least bit. And even though this is a relatively small segment, it still had a big impact on top-line results.

This decline in revenue for the company brought with it a decline in profitability as well. Net income went from $17.2 million to negative $2.3 million. Operating cash flow dropped from $70.1 million to $37.1 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $49.4 million to $9.7 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company fell from $47.7 million to $15.1 million. In the chart above, you can also see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Revenue, profits, and cash flows are all lower.

It is worth mentioning the role that tariffs have had on the company. During the first nine months of this year, they negatively impacted profitability to the tune of $60 million. And $40 million of that hit came in the third quarter on its own. It has only been because of higher prices that the company has not experienced more downside because of tariffs. And unfortunately, management expects that tariff levels will have an even larger impact next year. And on top of this, their own expectation for softer market demand, and you can understand why I am pessimistic right now.

Long term, management has high expectations for the company. They have not established any specific timeline that I can find. But they hope that revenue will grow from $3.9 billion on a trailing 12-month basis as of the end of the third quarter of this year to $5 billion or greater at some point in the future. If this comes to fruition, operating cash flow will nearly double, rising from $110 million to over $300 million. And in that vein, the company intends to invest heavily in additional acquisition activities and innovation. One thing that will help is that management is actively working on cost-savings plans. By 2027, they expect to cut annual operating expenses by between $30 million and $40 million. So that should help some.

In other articles, I have detailed my concerns about the state of the economy. I firmly believe that the U.S. is heading toward a recession. That's why, even with the trading multiples that we have right now, I am not terribly optimistic about Hyster-Yale. Based on expectations for this year, EBITDA should come in at around $90 million, while adjusted operating cash flow should be around $83 million. Using these estimates and also using the historical results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above precisely how the company is valued. These multiples aren't bad. And in fact, as the table below illustrates, shares are actually cheap compared to the five other enterprises that I stacked the business up against. But there is another issue that causes me concern. And that is none other than backlog.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Hyster-Yale 7.3 11.2
Kornit Digital (KRNT) 15.6 508.1
Columbus McKinnon Corporation (CMCO) 10.9 11.4
Graham Corporation (GHM) 53.7 30.1
Energy Recovery (ERII) 38.6 25.9
Stratasys (SSYS) 98.8 59.1

During the third quarter of this year, backlog for the company came in at $1.35 billion. This compares to the $2.30 billion the company reported a year ago. This is a huge drop and is indicative of incredibly weak end market demand for its offerings. It's also a decline from what the company saw in the second quarter. That can be seen in the chart below.

The good news is that we are starting to see some improvement in bookings. The third quarter of this year saw bookings of $380 million. That was up from a $370 million a year earlier. And for the first nine months of this year, the $1.30 billion of bookings is above the $1.27 billion recorded a year earlier. But still, even though that does indicate some stabilization, we need to see a growth or at least a stabilization and backlog before I become neutral or bullish.

Takeaway

As things stand, I believe that investors should be wise to be cautious about this particular prospect. Given the state of the economy and the overall financial health of the business, I think that there could be some additional pain around the corner. If we do start to see backlog stabilize, or even better, grow, it wouldn't take much for me to upgrade it. But for now, I think that maintaining it as a Sell candidate makes sense.

data.title: Fidelity Real Estate Investment Portfolio Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

For the third quarter of 2025, the U.S. real estate investment trust market, as measured by the MSCI U.S. IMI Real Estate 25/50 Linked Index, returned 3.76%, lagging the 8.12% gain of the U.S. stock market, according to the S&P 500® index. Economically sensitive property segments – such as regional malls (+18%), industrial (+7%), office (+7%) and lodging real estate (+6%) investment trusts – outperformed in this environment, given the companies' more cyclical cash flows and as the market proved more accommodating toward property types tied to near-term economic growth.

In contrast, residential REITs – which includes apartment (-4%) and single-family rental (-9%) – and telecom tower REITs (-11%) were among the relative laggards, reflecting investors' collective expectation that cash flows may not accelerate in the current market environment. The underperformance of residential real estate also reflected market participants' concern about a near-term excess in supply, particularly in the Sun Belt region, where significant building activity has taken place. However, property fundamentals in the office real estate segment appear to have bottomed, with "green shoots" appearing in leasing activity.

Meanwhile, the U.S. equity market was strong in Q3, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April. The advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reduction since December. Growth stocks continued to lead in Q3, while smaller-cap shares outpaced large-caps, signaling a broadening of market leadership and increased investor appetite for risk. Renewed expectations for Fed rate cuts also supported stocks. On September 17, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points, shifting from its wait-and-see stance. The decision reflected concerns about labor-market softness, which outweighed a rise in inflation – up 2.9% year-over-year in August, the highest reading since January. As of September 30, the market expects monetary easing into 2026 and beyond. ■

Performance Review

For the third quarter, the fund gained 0.89%, significantly trailing the 3.76% advance of the benchmark, the MSCI U.S. IMI Real Estate 25/50 Linked Index.

Versus the real estate industry index, security selection in the industrial REITs segment meaningfully detracted. In this group, an overweight stake in Americold Realty Trust (-25%), an owner and operator of warehouse properties specializing in the cold storage of frozen and fresh foods, was a particularly notable detractor. Lower-than-expected utilization of the firm's facilities weighed on the stock. Meanwhile, the fund's overweight in Prologis (+10%) – the world's largest industrial REIT and the No. 1 portfolio holding as of September 30 – partly offset the negative security selection within the industrial category. Picks within retail REITs also detracted from the fund's relative performance. Most notably, lacking exposure to strong-performing mall owner Simon Property Group (+18%) weighed on the fund's relative result. Instead, we chose to gain most of our retail exposure through shopping centers, which we saw as a superior business to malls. Stock picking and an overweight in the telecom tower category further hampered results, especially an overweight stake in American Tower (-12%) – the fund's No. 2 holding on September 30. As relatively defensive investments, tower stocks largely lagged their more economically sensitive counterparts this quarter.

On the positive side, the fund benefited from a relative overweight in Compass (+27%), which operates an online real estate brokerage platform. This company benefited from strong operational management, high agent productivity and the effective use of technology to boost business. We reduced the fund's allocation to this stock in Q3. Also boosting the fund's relative result was a lack of exposure to apartment REIT AvalonBay Communities (-4%). Apartment REITs were relative underperformers in Q3, reflecting the market's apparent view that cash flows for these REITs may not accelerate in the current economic environment. ■

Outlook and Positioning

Our investment strategy in Q3 emphasized our best stock picking ideas based on individual company fundamentals and valuations. As of September 30, modest outsized exposure to apartment REITs (9%) reflected our view that the industry is well positioned over the long term. Even in the heavily built Sunbelt region, we believe that new supply is likely to decline over time. We also believe this trend is likely to persist, given higher construction costs, restrictive immigration policies leading to rising labor rates and massive demand for data centers that is further distorting labor supply. All told, it's adding delays to the construction process, which could further constrain supply and push up rents over time.

Elsewhere, we maintained substantial exposure to industrial REITs (16% of the fund at quarter end and a sizable overweight) due to strong demand for specialized distribution facilities, driven in part by rapid growth in e-commerce. Although industrial REITs encountered difficulty earlier in 2025, we expect to see occupancy rates start to tick up as the supply/demand balance improves. The positive impact of this reduction will take time to play out, but we believe it ultimately could be quite beneficial to industrial REIT cash flows, as property owners will need higher rental rates to economically justify new construction. Another area of opportunity that we continue to see is the assisted-living industry, which is benefiting from a favorable combination of strong demand and constrained supply. In Q3, the fund's allocation to health care REITs was 12%, up from about 11% at midyear. Meanwhile, the fund maintained limited exposure to mall owners, given the significant long-term business challenges they face.

However, we did see attractive opportunities among retail strip-center REITs and, as such, maintained an overweight in the industry. As we see it, owners of strip centers have better businesses than mall owners, benefiting from favorable demand and more limited supply of attractive locations. The fund is overweight data centers – which made up about 12% of the fund on September 30. It's become increasingly challenging to satisfy the demand for data centers due to constraints on new supply, which include access to power and water resources and the scarcity of optimal locations. Many customers want to be close to the internet's on- and off ramps to make their data connections faster. Thus, well-located data centers are seeing strong growth in rental income. This situation is creating tremendous pricing power for the industry. In seeking data center industry investments, we've prioritized REITs that operate in desirable locations close to their customers, as well as those with stronger financial positions and greater access to capital.

Our outlook for REITs is generally positive. Market participants are anticipating several additional cuts to short-term interest rates – a positive factor if it also leads to lower long-term rates and, as a result, the cost of capital. In addition, after the REIT market's significant underperformance in recent years, valuations have become much more attractive relative to the broader equity market. In short, we're relatively optimistic about REITs, though we're also mindful of how growth-oriented equities seemingly continue to go up daily. In this environment, one should remember that REITs have historically been good diversifiers of a growth-heavy portfolio. All told, we're going to continue to pay close attention to the broader economic context in which the fund operates, while focusing our attention on bottom-up stock picking. ■

Investment Approach

Fidelity® Real Estate Investment Portfolio seeks above-average income and long-term capital growth, consistent with reasonable investment risk, by investing in securities of companies that own and, in most cases, operate commercial real estate properties.

Investment in real estate securities has the potential to provide portfolio diversification, consistent income generation, total return and the ability to outpace inflation.

We believe real estate investment trusts (REITS) represent a balance between real estate and stocks, and that recognizing attributes of both is key to identifying opportunities to outperform.

Through rigorous bottom-up research from both Fidelity's dedicated real estate team and the firm's broader research resources, we strive to add value through security selection within a disciplined risk framework. Our process seeks to determine the relative attractiveness of individual REITs and will try to take advantage of pricing discrepancies in the market.

data.title: Starfighters Space: A Hypersonic And Space Launch Hype To Sell
data.description:
Starfighters Space, Inc. (FJET) went public earlier this month, raising $40 million at $3.59 per share, including pre-sold common shares. The stock price subsequently rocketed to $31.50 and currently trades at $12.40 after the stock lost 60% of its value. In this report, I discuss the company’s operations, the IPO, and why I believe Starfighters Space is a typical name that has substantial risk attached despite space and hypersonic opportunities.

Starfighter Space: A Different View On Space Launch Systems

The conventional way to bring satellites into orbit is by launching a rocket from the ground consisting of various stages, and some of these stages are reusable, thereby lowering launch costs. Starfighter Space envisions a very different approach by utilizing F-104 supersonic jets that fly to 45,000 feet, release a rocket, which can be used to inject a microsatellite into orbit using the Starlaunch I vehicle and later on Small-sats using the Starlaunch II. The company expects that this air-launched method will substantially reduce launch costs per kilogram compared to peers. Lowering the cost-burden is a key requirement to unlock opportunities in space, and Starfighters Space aims to do that using the air-launch method.

At the same time, we should note that while this may lower costs and increase flexibility, prior attempts by Virgin Orbit to provide air-launched solutions have ended in failure, with the company ultimately collapsing. So, air-launched solutions are not new, nor is there a particularly successful track record, and that also does mean that I consider Starfighters Space to be a higher-risk investment for their space business. The company also quotes launch cost per kilogram using the Starlaunch II and it is more than likely that Starlaunch I costs per kilogram are worse than Starlaunch II costs. At a 100-kilogram payload to LEO, it is sized substantially smaller than Electron’s 150 kilogram, but it is not quite known whether there is any substantial demand for the payload class, nor is anything about the business concept proven. Rocket Lab’s (RKLB) Electron rocket enjoys a good track record of launch success and precision on orbit injection. I believe a major risk is that the Starlaunch I is not cost competitive, and the Starlaunch II initially may also not be cost competitive.

Besides air-launched satellite deployment, Starfighters Space offers pilot training for the F-104. The more promising part of the business, in my view, is the participation opportunities in hypersonic rocket testing for which the company has partnered with the Air Force Research Lab. Starfighters Space still has to prove its business case, grow a backlog, and realize target costs and revenues for profitability and mission success.

A Challenging Path For Starfighters Space

The IPO is labeled as if it raised $40 million, and that is only partially correct. The company started raising funds in September 2024, and prior to the IPO, it had issued around 5 million shares, leaving 6.15 million shares to be issued. The IPO in December resulted in gross proceeds of $22.1 million and $20.45 million in net proceeds. The company expected net proceeds of $36.2 million (Prospectus Page 42), $8 million is earmarked for CapEx, $4 million for Starlaunch related R&D, and $0.77 million for loan repayment.

The company’s expenditures have been loaded into the second half of 2025, with $12 million allocated to airplane acquisition, $1.5 million for a drop test to acquire a launch license from the FAA, and $5 million to acquire permission to launch. That brings the total backloaded costs to $18.5 million. So, much of the raised capital actually already has been assigned.

According to the cash flow statement, the annualized operating cash burn is $6.7 million. The company ended Q3 with $3.9 million in cash. Starfighters Space is currently in the development phase, meaning that it is generating no revenues and producing substantial operating losses. Starfighters Space also converted debt to equity on the 17th and 18th of December. I have mapped the pro forma cash position excluding the non-recurring costs, and that shows that there is a $23.4 million cash position. That does seem to be quite comfortable against the $6.7 million annualized cash burn. However, if we include the higher cost profile as the company needs to make investments to accelerate commercialization, we see that against expected costs to be carried this year, the year-end cash balance will only be $4.1 million.

Essentially that means that substantially more capital is needed, which increases the likelihood of further dilution. Currently, the stock is trading at around $12.40 while the cash per share by year-end is expected to be $0.14. Assuming the lack of substantial operating revenues and activities, the cash per share closely resembles the book value per share. Aerospace and defense peers trade at 3.7x P/B. A company such as Rocket Labs has a median of 5x while it currently trades at around 30 times its book value. A peer group valuation gives a $0.52 price target, while the RKLB median gives $0.70 and the current multiple gives a $4.20 target.

If we assume that the company will raise around $17.5 million to cover normalized cash usage for the coming two years, we get to $0.55 in cash per share, which, against the previously discussed multiples, is a price target of $2-$2.78 for the peer group and RKLB median and $16.65 against the current P/B for Rocket Lab. The current share price is $12.40, and I strongly believe that does not reflect the cash intensity in the quarters ahead and the unproven business model for Starfighters Space. As a result, I am marking the stock a Strong Sell.

Conclusion: Starfighters Space Stock Is Not Attractive At This Point

I believe that the Starfighters Space business concept is not an attractive one. On paper, the company can cover micro- and small satellite launches at a substantially lower launch cost per kg. However, we note that air launches have not been particularly successful, as they are also not easy to execute. Virgin Orbit, which used a bigger airplane and a bigger launcher, ended up failing as a flexible air-launch provider, and Starfighters Space has no proven track record on mission success and financial targets and timelines. The capital raise allows FJET to ramp up its efforts, but I believe additional capital raises are required, and consequently I do not believe there is an attractive investment angle for the company.

data.title: Japan Is The Canary In The Global Debt Coal Mine
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Investors are beginning to zero in on what is happening in Japan.

For decades, Japan sat in a world of near-zero rates, suppressed volatility, and chronic deflation, but that has quickly changed.

In a previous macro post, I discussed exactly how the yen carry unwind could threaten global markets.

But this is just one side of the coin.

The problem with Japan is structural debt and inflation, an issue which could soon plague other developed markets, too, including the US

Japan may just be the first domino to fall.

But what does this mean for investors?

The Yield Signal Investors Are Watching

Japanese yields are on the rise.

Some investors believe that if Japanese yields were ever to fully converge with or even exceed U.S. yields, this could "break" global bond markets.

But why?

Japan has built its entire fiscal and monetary framework on the assumption of permanently low interest rates. This monetary experiment may be coming to an end.

Japan’s Debt Problem Is Structural, Not Cyclical

Japan’s debt sits at over 200% of GDP.

Meanwhile, in the U.S., debt to GDP is closer to 120%.

While both figures are historically elevated and problematic, the difference in scale is critical.

A government services its debt through tax revenues generated by its economy. When debt grows far faster than economic output, even modest increases in interest rates can overwhelm fiscal capacity.

In Japan’s case, a significant portion of annual government spending already goes toward debt servicing. Higher yields quickly crowd out everything else.

Inflation: The Wrench In The Works

For more than 20 years, Japan struggled with deflation. This allowed the government to borrow aggressively at ultra-low rates with little immediate consequence.

That macro backdrop has now changed. Over the past three years, Japanese inflation has averaged roughly 3%.

While that number may look tame compared to recent U.S. or European experience, it is a profound shift for Japan.

Low inflation made aggressive monetary easing safe and ultimately sat at the heart of the now infamous Yen carry trade.

The Bank of Japan’s Dilemma

For years, the Bank of Japan acted as the buyer of last resort for Japanese government bonds, suppressing yields through massive quantitative easing. At its peak, the central bank owned a staggering share of the entire JGB market.

Once inflation appeared, this strategy became unsustainable, especially with the Yen weakening too.

Printing money to buy bonds in an inflationary environment risks currency instability and loss of confidence. As a result, the Bank of Japan has been forced to step back and, more recently, even reduce the size of its balance sheet.

Logically, when the largest buyer exits a market, prices fall, and yields rise.

Can Japan Grow Out of Its Debt?

This is the key question.

There is a credible argument that Japan could reduce its debt burden through inflation by growing nominal GDP, but the country faces some challenges.

Because Japan’s debt is overwhelmingly yen-denominated and domestically held, policymakers have more control than countries dependent on foreign capital.

If inflation could be sustained in the 2–3% range, nominal GDP growth would rise, tax revenues would increase, and the real value of existing debt would gradually erode.

But Japan’s structural growth is low due to an ageing population, weak productivity growth, and limited immigration.

Inflation without real growth risks raising costs without meaningfully expanding the tax base.

At the same time, rising bond yields threaten the entire strategy.

Japan can only inflate its way out if interest rates remain below nominal growth, and markets are now pushing back.

If yields rise too far, this could lead to renewed financial repression. The BoJ could start buying bonds again, or commercial banks could even be required to carry more sovereign debt.

Why This Matters for the United States

There are two key reasons why this matters to the U.S and U.S. investors.

Japan is the largest buyer of Treasuries, holding more than $1 trillion.

A destabilization of Japan’s bond markets could spillover into U.S. markets.

Namely, higher Japanese yields could encourage domestic investors to repatriate capital, selling U.S. Treasuries and buying JGBs.

This is an extension of the dynamics of the carry trade dynamics I discussed before.

But perhaps more important is the fact that Japan is basically showing us what the future could look like for the U.S.

While America isn't as indebted, deficits are a problem, and I do think that the government will try to reduce this debt burden through inflation.

Japan will be patient zero, as it tries to do just this.

  • Will they have to resort to more financial repression?

  • What does this even look like in a world with Gold and Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

  • And perhaps on a broader level, is there a way back from an ageing population operating below replacement rates?

What This Means For Investors

The market is already pricing in the endgame here.

The rally in gold (XAUUSD:CUR) in terms of U.S. dollars is nothing compared to the rally we have seen relative to the Japanese yen.

What’s interesting, though, is that even in the face of higher yields, the Nikkei (NKY:IND) has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500).

On the one hand, inflation has actually revived the Japanese economy, with hopes that wages and earnings will now begin to increase.

And of course, earnings will increase at least in nominal terms, as the yen keeps devaluing.

This is why, ultimately, as governments around the world try to crawl their way out of debt, assets, both hard and financial, will do well.

data.title: It Will Likely Be A Good Year For FSK And Its Yield-Investors
data.description:
FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK) was one of my first business development companies, or BDCs, that I included in my portfolio, back in late 2023. It was also one of the first BDCs that I covered on this platform, issuing a bullish article in December 2023. In that piece I compared FSK (at that time the second largest BDC) with Ares Capital (ARCC) (the largest BDC), and made a decision to go with the former due to a more compelling P/NAV multiple relative to the underlying NII generation.

Since then I was holding FSK in my portfolio all the time until January this year, when I published a thesis update piece. My decision was to harvest ~27% in total return profits and exit FSK due to a too marginal discount, which had then narrowed to only 7%. I just felt that the odds for an upside were much lower than seeing a leg down.

The timing was almost perfect. After that decision and until I decided to reopen the stake in FSK, the share price had dropped by ~33%.

If we measure returns from the moment when I deployed again capital into FSK (October 9, 2025), we will notice that the total return figure is almost 6%, which even slightly exceeds that of the overall BDC sector (BIZD).

However, as of now, FSK still trades at a very steep discount of 32%, which is more or less at the same level when I made my first move in FSK. I have to say that the situation looks quite attractive on a risk-adjusted basis due to exactly the massive discount. It is clear that the fundamentals are not the strongest out there for FSK, but I would argue that the disconnect is simply unjustified given how some other peers are priced (e.g., sector average is 0.86x).

Let's quickly review the case and see how the recent Q3 financials have impacted the story here.

Thesis review

I will immediately start with the challenges, which are quite many.

First, the NII per share declined in Q3 to $0.57 (down by $0.03 compared to the previous quarter). The key drivers were largely the same as for most other BDC peers - i.e., lower base rates and tighter spreads. In the earlier quarters of 2025, FSK was battling with rising non-accruals, which added an extra layer of pressure for the NII per share generation. In Q3, 2025, this component was well-managed.

Second, based on the most recent 10-Q filing, FSK has approximately 15% of income coming in the form of payment in kind. The sector average is 6.4% and for some top-notch quality BDCs the PIK gets even lower than that - e.g., for Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) and Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC). The reason why FSK has so meaningful exposure to PIK stems mostly from the multiple restructured loans, where the BDC has tailored the financings in order to avoid full losses.

Third, FSK's base dividend coverage stands at 89%, which is a clear sign that the days until the chopping block moment arrives are not that many left. The case for a dividend cut is also 'supported' by the previously mentioned PIK, which is a non-cash income, making the actual dividend coverage even worse. Furthermore, while FSK has some debt capacity available (debt to equity of 1.16x), the room is not that big to secure incremental NII per share growth just through debt-driven portfolio expansion.

Let's also remember that there are lagged effects from the Fed's base rate cuts in October, November and December that have not been absorbed in the Q3 data and will gradually percolate through Q4 and Q1, 2025 books. Namely, NII per share is very likely headed downwards.

So, this is, in a nutshell, the reason why the market is discounting FSK so heavily.

But having said that, I still think that the discount is too harsh and we should be able to profit nicely from FSK in 2026. Here is the thing.

Currently, FSK offers a dividend yield of 19.2%. Let's imagine that the Management will announce a cut by reducing the dividend by 40% in order to emerge out from the underfunded dividend's status (on a cash basis) and still leave about 10% of surplus NII per share generation that would allow to absorb the forthcoming pressure from lower interest rates. In that case, FSK's base dividend coverage would be close to 130% and 115% on a PIK adjusted basis, which is way above the sector average dividend coverage of 100%.

Because of the deep discount and elevated current yield, we would still be left with enticing current income streams of ~11.5%.

Now, the key is what happens with non-accruals going forward. In this context, the following comment by the Management in Q3 earnings call provides an important information:

We also believe it is helpful to provide the market with information based upon FSK's assets originated by KKR Credit. Nonaccruals relating to the 90% of the portfolio, which has been originated by KKR Credit and the FS/KKR Advisor, were 3.4% on a cost basis and 1.8% on a fair value basis as of the end of the third quarter. This compares to 3.8% on a cost basis and 2% on a fair value basis as of the end of the second quarter.

Many investors might not have noticed it, but the main source of FSK's exploding non-accruals has been the externally acquired portfolio, which has finally largely churned out, leaving smaller and smaller risk base from which unfavorable news could emerge. For instance, the current KKR Credit originated/underwritten portfolio non-accrual statistic is actually lower than for the publicly traded BDC sector average (closer to 2.5%). Finally, another element, which should help FSK is the lower base rate environment - i.e., positive for FSK investment companies, which now enjoy cheaper debt service than before the rates started to move down.

The bottom line

I want to make it clear that FSK is not your "buy and forget" case. I have not included it in my core BDC portfolio bucket, where there are such resilient gems as KBDC, Fidus Investment (FDUS) and Trinity Capital (TRIN). Instead, I have placed FSK in my tactical BDC portfolio bucket, which consumes much smaller percentage of the total exposure and where high risk, high reward plays can be made.

FSK's case really boils down to non-accruals. If FSK can finally showcase to the market that the worst days are over and that the current portfolio is largely cleansed from externally acquired trojan horses, then even after a dividend cut, I see significant upside from P/NAV convergence to the sector average (and all of this would be supported by post-cut double digit yield).

Seeing the recent data points on the portfolio and non-accrual front, I am willing to take that bet.

data.title: Apple Doesn't Need A Stronger AI Portfolio
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) has consistently been one of the largest tech companies in the world, worth more than $4 trillion. The company has consistently benefited from the dominance of its hardware platforms, although there are market concerns around its AI positioning. Despite that, Apple is continuing to be a substantial platform for AI with continued growth.

Apple Financial Results

Apple managed to earn impressive financial results in the most recent quarter.

The company crossed more than $100 billion in quarterly revenue a first for a non year-ending quarter (traditionally its largest). The company managed to achieve high single-digit YoY revenue growth, incredible given its scale, and it did that with total cost of sales at only $54 billion. That means roughly 52% of the company's revenue is cost of sales.

That's incredibly impressive for a primarily hardware company. At the same time, unlike other tech companies, Apple has comfortably managed investing in artificial intelligence, without blowing out any semblance of a budget. The company's R&D expenses have expanded by combined with G&A the segment is still at less than $16 billion / year.

The net result is after paying taxes, Apple had net income of $27.5 billion. That's more than $100 billion in annualized net income for the giant, net income that it continues to rapidly grow.

Apple Platform Dominance

At the same time, those who're afraid of the impact of artificial intelligence would do well to note Apple's status as a platform.

The top free iPhone app is currently ChatGPT. That application that everyone is concerned about replacing iPhone is being used through the iPhone. Similarly, the same people using ChatGPT online are using it through Mac. The same through coding tools like Claude AI etc. which are being run on the same laptops programmers were already using.

This is key in our view, because offloading compute for tasks to large language models, or LLMs, could potentially even reduce people's needs for beefy local compute enabling them to shift from Desktops to personal computing such as Laptops.

Apple Segment Breakdown

From a segment perspective, the Apple thesis remains strong with great performance across the board.

The company only saw revenue decline in the Greater China, where Apple is facing strong domestic competition. However, even here, the company's revenue decline was in the low single-digits. Everywhere outside of the market where there's concerted effort to limit the company in favor of domestic alternatives is seeing high single-digit to double-digit growth.

Apple is seeing strength in categories as well. While the iPad and Wearables categories remained flat, the company saw strong iPhone and Mac growth, the core categories that lead to follow-on growth with other categories. Apple's services business also remained incredibly strong. This is another sign of Apple's strength.

Apple Shareholder Return Potential

Since starting its shareholder return program, Apple managed to reducing its outstanding shares by 43%.

Apple has spent more than $700 billion to purchase these shares, or $64 / share. That's a quarter of current prices. Apple recently announced an extra $100 billion buyback after a $110 billion buyback last year. The company has a very modest dividend of <0.4%, but with buybacks total shareholder returns are roughly 3% annualized.

It's modest, but for a company growing at the high single-digits, it's still very respectable. The company's growth will enable increasing buybacks and shareholder returns and enable the company to expand into its P/E of ~30x that goes virtually entirely to shareholders.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is Apple's valuation. Apple is sitting right at a $4 trillion market capitalization, with $112 billion in TTM net income. That puts the company at a P/E of ~30x. In a difficult market, Apple needs continued net income growth to justify its valuation, and whether or not that happens remains to be seen.

Conclusion

Apple is one of the largest companies in the world with a $4 trillion market capitalization. The company has managed to shrink its shares outstanding by almost 50% over the past 13 years and it's focused on continuing to improve that. The company is spending almost $10 billion per month on share repurchases an indication of its massive scale.

The company is achieving high single-digit growth with strength on every segment of its platform. In addition, the company is the core platform that people are using for their AI usage, whether through ChatGPT or AI coding. Going forward, this makes Apple a valuable long-term investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.
